# Abstract
This study examines postcolonial narrative strategies in late twentieth century novels. We focus on voice, temporality, and memory.

# Introduction
Postcolonial literature often contests dominant historical accounts. Narrative form becomes a site of resistance.

# Methods
We performed close readings of four novels and coded narrative shifts. Themes were triangulated with archival commentary.

# Results
The texts employ fragmented chronology and polyvocal narration. These strategies foreground marginalized perspectives.

Figure 1. Conceptual map of narrative strategies.

# Discussion
Fragmentation functions as both aesthetic choice and political critique. The findings connect to broader debates in cultural memory.

Table 1. Summary of narrative devices across novels.

# Conclusion
Narrative experimentation enables counter histories within postcolonial fiction. Further work may include comparative translations.

# References
[1] G. Scholar. Memory and narrative in postcolonial studies. 2016.
[2] H. Scholar. Voice and temporality in fiction. 2014.
